flashcards.front: What is the time complexity of Merge Sort in worst, average, and best cases?
flashcards.back: O(n log n) for all three cases. This is because Merge Sort always divides the array into halves and takes linear time to merge them.

flashcards.front: What is the space complexity of Merge Sort?
flashcards.back: O(n) auxiliary space is required. This is because merging two sorted arrays needs additional space equal to the size of the original array.

flashcards.front: Merge Sort is a _______ algorithm based on _______ technique.
flashcards.back: Merge Sort is a stable sorting algorithm based on the divide-and-conquer technique.

flashcards.front: Who invented Merge Sort and when?
flashcards.back: Merge Sort was invented by John von Neumann in 1945.

flashcards.front: What are the two main phases of Merge Sort?
flashcards.back: The two main phases are: 1) DIVIDE phase - recursively divide the array into subarrays until each subarray has one element, and 2) CONQUER phase - merge the sorted subarrays back together.

flashcards.front: Why is Merge Sort considered stable?
flashcards.back: Merge Sort is stable because it maintains the relative order of equal elements. During merging, when elements are equal, the element from the left subarray is placed first.

flashcards.front: What is the recurrence relation for Merge Sort?
flashcards.back: T(n) = 2T(n/2) + O(n), where T(1) = O(1). Solving this gives T(n) = O(n log n).

flashcards.front: What is the key operation in Merge Sort?
flashcards.back: The MERGE operation is key - it takes two sorted subarrays and combines them into one sorted array using an auxiliary array.

flashcards.front: Why does Merge Sort require O(n) extra space?
flashcards.back: The merge step requires an auxiliary array of size n to temporarily store elements while merging two sorted subarrays.

flashcards.front: Compare Merge Sort and Quick Sort in terms of worst-case behavior.
flashcards.back: Quick Sort can degrade to O(n²) in worst case (unbalanced partitions), while Merge Sort guarantees O(n log n) in all cases.
True in-place Merge Sort is difficult to implement efficiently. Standard Merge Sort is not in-place due to O(n) auxiliary space requirement.

flashcards.front: How many comparisons does Merge Sort make in the worst case?
flashcards.back: In the worst case, Merge Sort makes approximately n log₂ n - n + 1 comparisons, which is optimal for comparison-based sorting.

flashcards.front: What is the base case for Merge Sort recursion?
flashcards.back: The base case is when the subarray has only one element (or zero elements). A single element is already sorted.

flashcards.front: Why is Merge Sort not adaptive?
flashcards.back: Merge Sort is not adaptive because it performs the same operations regardless of whether the input is already sorted or completely random.
